Mathematical Foundations of AI & ML
Unit 7: Generalization, Bias-Variance, and Model Selection

Prof. Dr. Philipp Pelz

FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg

01. Title: Generalization, Bias-Variance, and Model Selection

  • The Integrity Challenge: Anyone can fit a line to points. Can you build a model that works on data you haven’t seen yet?
  • Unit Role: Establishing the scientific rigor required to claim a model is “useful.”
  • Goal: Master the diagnostics and protocols that separate science from lucky guessing.

02. Learning outcomes

By the end of this unit, students can: - Decompose prediction error into Bias, Variance, and Irreducible Noise. - Design robust Cross-Validation pipelines that prevent selection bias. - Detect and mitigate various forms of Data Leakage in materials workflows. - Interpret Learning Curves to guide data collection and model scaling.

03. Recap from Unit 6

  • Optimization: We found the local minimum of the training loss.
  • Pathology: We saw that optimizers can find extremely complex solutions that have zero training error.
  • Question: Is a model with zero training error actually “better”?

04. Training vs. Test Error

  • Training Error: How well the model fits the data it was given.
  • Test Error: How well the model predicts new data from the same process.
  • The Generalization Gap: \(E_{test} - E_{train}\). A large gap is the signature of Overfitting.

05. The IID Assumption and its Limits

  • IID: Independent and Identically Distributed.
  • We assume our training and test samples come from the same distribution.
  • In Materials Science: This often breaks!
    • Train on data from machine A, test on machine B.
    • Train on one crystal family, test on another.
    • Shift awareness is mandatory (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

06. Underfitting vs. Overfitting

  • Underfitting (High Bias): Model is too rigid to see the pattern. (e.g., linear line for quadratic data).
  • Overfitting (High Variance): Model is so flexible it “memorizes” the noise.
  • Visual: The classic “U-shaped” curve of Test Error vs. Complexity.

07. Bias-Variance Decomposition (Mathematical)

  • For a regression model \(\hat{f}\), the expected error at \(x\) is: \[ \mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]^2 + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2 \]
  • Bias: \((\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x))^2\).
  • Variance: \(\mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]\).
  • \(\sigma^2\): Irreducible noise (Bayes error) (Murphy 2012).

08. The High Bias Regime

  • Symptoms: High training error and high test error.
  • Remedies:
    • Increase model complexity (more layers, more neurons).
    • Add more features (feature engineering).
    • Decrease regularization strength.

09. The High Variance Regime

  • Symptoms: Low training error but high test error.
  • Remedies:
    • Add more training data.
    • Increase regularization (L1, L2, Dropout).
    • Use simpler models or reduce the number of features.

10. Learning Curves: The Diagnostic Powerhouse

  • Plot Error vs. Number of Training Samples.
  • Underfit: Curves converge quickly but at a high error level. Adding data doesn’t help.
  • Overfit: Large gap between curves. Adding data usually helps close the gap (Neuer et al. 2024).

11. The Validation Set Purpose

  • The Problem: If we use the Test Set to tune \(\lambda\) or depth, we are “fitting” the test set.
  • The Solution: Split data into Train (to fit \(w\)), Validation (to tune hyperparameters), and Test (for final, honest reporting).

12. Cross-Validation Basics (k-fold)

  • Partition data into \(k\) non-overlapping segments (“folds”).
  • Train \(k\) times, each time using one fold as validation and the rest as training.
  • Benefit: More stable estimate of performance; every data point gets to be in the “validation” set exactly once.

13. Nested Cross-Validation

  • Goal: Avoid “optimistic bias” when comparing dozens of different model types.
  • Inner Loop: Hyperparameter tuning.
  • Outer Loop: Final performance estimation.
  • Rule: If you use CV to pick a model, you need an outer layer to test that choice.

14. Data Leakage: The Silent Killer

  • Leakage: When information from the “future” or the test set sneaks into the training process.
  • Example: Normalizing the entire dataset before splitting (the mean/std of the test set is now known by the trainer!).
  • Result: “God-like” performance in the lab, total failure in the field.

15. Hyperparameter Tuning Protocol

  • Grid Search: Exhaustive search over a fixed grid (slow).
  • Random Search: Surprisingly better! Higher probability of finding the “narrow” peaks of good performance.
  • Bayesian Optimization: Efficiently explores the space by building a “meta-model” of the accuracy (Murphy 2012).

16. Regularization and Generalization (Link)

  • Regularization acts as a “damper” on variance.
  • By penalizing large weights, we restrict the model to a “flatter” hypothesis space that is less likely to zig-zag between noisy points.

17. Early Stopping as Model Selection

  • Every iteration of SGD is a “model variant.”
  • Early stopping picks the variant that performs best on the validation set.
  • Intuition: We stop the optimizer before it has time to start fitting the tiny, noisy details.

18. Feature Selection Pitfalls

  • Leakage: Selecting features based on their correlation with \(y\) using the entire dataset.
  • Solution: Feature selection must be inside the Cross-Validation loop.

19. Model Complexity Measures (VC Dimension)

  • Vapnik-Chervonenkis (VC) Dimension: Measures the capacity of a model to “shatter” (classify perfectly) any set of \(N\) points.
  • Principle: Simple models have low VC-dim and are less likely to overfit.

20. Occam’s Razor and MDL

  • Minimum Description Length (MDL): The best model is the one that minimizes the sum of (length of the model code) + (length of the error code).
  • Simple models are easier to communicate and more likely to be true (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

21. Distribution Shift Problem

  • Covariate Shift: \(p(x)\) changes, but \(p(y|x)\) stays the same.
  • Concept Shift: \(p(y|x)\) changes (the rules of the game changed).
  • Materials Context: A model trained on high-purity samples may fail on low-cost, industrial-grade materials.

22. Domain Adaptation (Preview)

  • Techniques to “re-calibrate” a model to a new distribution with minimal new labels.
  • We will see more of this in the ML-PC course!

23. Calibration and Decision Quality

  • Is 90% accuracy enough?
  • If the model is 90% accurate but 100% confident every time, it is uncalibrated.
  • Risk: Overconfidence in wrong predictions leads to catastrophic failures in engineering.

24. Uncertainty and Model Choice

  • Sometimes the “best” model is not the most accurate one, but the one that “knows what it doesn’t know.”
  • Epistemic Uncertainty: High where data is sparse. Our model selection should prioritize models that reflect this (Neuer et al. 2024).

25. Metrics Aligned to Objective

  • Accuracy: Misleading if classes are imbalanced.
  • F1-Score: Harmonic mean of precision and recall.
  • AUROC: Measures the quality of the “ranking” produced by the model.
  • Decision Utility: Assign monetary or safety costs to errors.

26. Class Imbalanced and Metrics

  • In materials discovery, “Success” is 1 in 10,000.
  • A model that always predicts “Failure” is 99.99% accurate but 0% useful.
  • Use Precision-Recall curves instead of accuracy.

27. Statistical Comparison of Models

  • Is Model A (\(92\%\)) really better than Model B (\(91\%\))?
  • Paired T-Tests: Check if the difference in CV scores is statistically significant.
  • Confidence Intervals: Report ranges of expected performance, not single numbers.

28. Multiple-Comparison Caution

  • If you test 100 random models, 5 will likely have “good” accuracy just by chance (\(p < 0.05\)).
  • Bonferroni Correction: Adjust your significance thresholds when performing many tests.

29. Ensembling Effects

  • Bagging (Random Forests): Reduces Variance by averaging many independent overfit models.
  • Boosting (XGBoost): Reduces Bias by sequentially training models to fix the errors of their predecessors (mcclarren2021machine?).

30. Stacking and Blending (Preview)

  • Using a “Meta-Model” to learn how to best combine the predictions of several base models.
  • Often the winning strategy in ML competitions (Kaggle).

31. Reproducibility Requirements

  • Seeds: Fix the random seed for shuffling and initialization.
  • Fixed Splits: Use hashes of filenames or IDs to ensure the same split across different machines.
  • Environment: Document library versions (PyTorch, Scikit-learn).

32. Benchmarking Hygiene

  • Prevent “Cherry-picking”: Only reporting the one split where your model did well.
  • Standardized Datasets: Use established benchmarks (Materials Project, Matbench) to compare against the community.

33. Model Cards and Reporting

  • A “Passport” for your model documenting:
    • Intended use.
    • Training data distribution.
    • Known failure modes.
    • Fairness and bias metrics.

34. Real-world Drift Monitoring

  • Once deployed in a factory, data changes.
  • Monitor: The distribution of model predictions. If it shifts significantly, the model may need retraining.

35. Continual Re-evaluation

  • Active Learning: Identify points where the model is uncertain and prioritize them for expert labeling.
  • Feedback Loop: Incorporate results of physical tests back into the training set.

36. Materials example: Sparse-data model choice

  • Task: Predicting lattice constants from 50 samples.
  • Decision: Use a simple Ridge Regression or Gaussian Process. A deep MLP would have massive variance and fail to generalize.

37. Materials example: Split by Chemistry Family

  • Random Split: Train on 80% of oxides, test on 20% of oxides. (High accuracy, low transfer).
  • Grouped Split: Train on oxides, test on nitrides. (Realistic check for materials discovery) (Sandfeld et al. 2024).

38. Materials example: Uncertainty-aware selection

  • Choose the model that has the smallest Expected Calibration Error (ECE).
  • This ensures that if the model predicts 80% probability of success, the material actually succeeds 80% of the time.

39. Common exam traps

40. Checklist: Robust Model Selection

41. Exercise 1 scaffold: Learning Curves

  • Implement a script that trains a model on 10, 20, 50, 100… samples.
  • Plot Train and Val error.
  • Identify at which point adding more data provides diminishing returns.

42. Exercise 2 scaffold: CV-based Comparison

  • Compare a Linear Model, a Decision Tree, and an MLP on a materials dataset.
  • Use 5-fold Cross-Validation.
  • Plot the mean and std-dev of the scores.

43. Exercise 3 scaffold: Leakage Detection

  • I will give you a “broken” script that achieves 100% accuracy.
  • Your task: Find where the test data leaked into the training (hint: check the preprocessing steps).

44. Exercise 4 scaffold: Calibration Report

  • Compute the reliability diagram for a classification model.
  • Calculate the ECE (Expected Calibration Error).
  • Apply Platt Scaling to fix the calibration and re-evaluate.

45. Exam-oriented key statements

  1. Generalization is the core challenge of ML.
  2. Bias-Variance tradeoff governs model complexity choice.
  3. Cross-validation provides a stable estimate of generalization.
  4. Leakage invalidates scientific claims.
  5. Accuracy is not a sufficient metric for imbalanced or high-stakes data.

46. Summary Table: Failure Modes

Symptom Diagnosis Mitigation
High Train Error High Bias More complex model, more features
Low Train, High Test High Variance More data, Regularization
100% Accuracy (too good) Leakage Check split and preprocessing
Good Accuracy, Bad Field Shift Re-evaluate IID assumptions

47. Unit summary

  • We’ve moved from “how to train” to “how to judge.”
  • Model selection is a statistical process that requires strict isolation of data.
  • Trust is built on transparent diagnostics and honest reporting.

48. Reading assignment

  • Read: Neuer Ch. 1.3 (CRISP-DM), Bishop Ch. 1.1, 1.3.
  • Preview: Probabilistic Models - moving from point estimates to entire distributions.

49. Transition Preview

  • Unit 8 will reframe everything we’ve learned through the lens of Probability.
  • We will see that “Regularization” is just “Prior Knowledge” in disguise.

50. References

  • Neuer (2024), Machine Learning for Engineers.
  • Sandfeld (2024), Materials Data Science.
  • Murphy (2012), Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective.
  • Bishop (2006), Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning.
Murphy, Kevin P. 2012. Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective. MIT Press.
Neuer, Michael et al. 2024. Machine Learning for Engineers: Introduction to Physics-Informed, Explainable Learning Methods for AI in Engineering Applications. Springer Nature.
Sandfeld, Stefan et al. 2024. Materials Data Science. Springer.
